strands.title: Research Question Crystallized — Demand Paging for LLM Context Windows
strands.content: Cycle 10 crystallizes the core research thesis: Pichay investigates the effect of REMOVING DEAD TOOL OUTPUTS from agentic LLM context windows. This is not merely a 'cache management' problem; it is a DEMAND PAGING problem adapted from classical OS memory management. The name 'Pichay' itself (Quechua: 'to sweep, to clean') signals the intellectual framing: cleaning dead tool outputs from context. The research artifact implements a MITM proxy between Claude Code (the IDE/client) and the Anthropic API (the LLM endpoint), instrumented with a FIFO eviction engine (pager.py) that selectively removes stale tool results from the agentic context. This is empirical research on a specific, high-impact problem: in agentic workflows, tool outputs accumulate rapidly (file reads, code execution results, API calls), bloating context windows and increasing inference cost. Pichay measures: (1) how much context can be freed by removing dead tool outputs, (2) does removing them degrade performance (agent effectiveness), (3) what is the cost/quality trade-off? The gateway CLI (pichay --claude, pichay --codex) instantiates a live proxy service with Prometheus metrics, health checks, session tracking, and live dashboard observability. This is not a simulation; it is a live experimental harness deployed in real agentic workflows.

key_claims.text: Pichay is a demand-paging framework for agentic LLM context windows, measuring the effect of removing dead tool outputs
The core instrumentation is a MITM proxy (proxy.py) between Claude Code and Anthropic API, with selective FIFO eviction (pager.py)
The gateway CLI provides live observability: Prometheus metrics, health checks, session tracking, and a live browser dashboard
Pichay is deployed in real agentic workflows (Claude Code integration), not simulation-only
The research question directly addresses cost reduction and performance trade-offs in agentic LLM inference

strands.title: Substantive Domain Grounding — From Repository Structure to Live Implementation
strands.content: Cycle 10 advances from structural observation (cycle 9) to concrete implementation details. The Pichay source layout (src/pichay/) reveals a tightly-scoped, modular architecture: proxy.py (MITM interception), pager.py (eviction engine), eval.py (metrics and cost analysis), __main__.py (experiment runner CLI). This is NOT a large, diffuse codebase; it is a focused research tool designed for a specific measurement problem. The README.md establishes conventions (dataclass patterns, match existing code style before writing), suggesting an active development culture. The paper reproduction pipeline (paper/reproduce_paper.sh, tools/reproduce_paper_counts.py) reveals scientific rigor: figures are regenerated from live snapshots (corpus_snapshot_YYYYMMDD_live.json), manuscript literals are checked against canonical data (dedup_by_content, min_size=10000), and LaTeX is rebuilt from clean state. The reference protocol (docs/corpus_protocol.md) standardizes counting and reporting. This is evidence of mature scientific methodology: results are reproducible, auditable, and version-controlled. The experiments/ directory is explicitly protected ("Do not delete experiment data"), indicating that this is where the scientific record lives.

key_claims.text: Pichay's source code is modular and focused: proxy.py, pager.py, eval.py, __main__.py
The paper reproduction pipeline ensures reproducibility: automated figure generation, literal-vs-snapshot checking, clean LaTeX rebuild
Canonical counting protocols (dedup_by_content, min_size=10000) enforce consistency in paper claims and experimental reporting
The experiments/ directory is the scientific record; explicit policy against deletion protects data integrity
The codebase uses dataclass patterns and consistent naming conventions, reflecting active, professional development

strands.title: Technical Architecture — MITM Proxy + FIFO Paging + Live Observability
strands.content: Cycle 10 reveals the three-tier technical architecture underlying Pichay's measurement capability. TIER 1 (Interception): The MITM proxy (proxy.py) sits between Claude Code (the IDE client) and the Anthropic API, capturing and instrumenting all request-response pairs in agentic workflows. This position allows observation of tool output sequences in real agent execution without modifying the client or server. TIER 2 (Eviction): The pager.py engine implements FIFO eviction logic: it tracks tool output liveness, identifies dead/stale outputs (those no longer referenced by recent context), and removes them before sending context to the LLM. FIFO is a baseline strategy; the research likely tests alternative eviction policies (LRU, LFU, utility-weighted) in the experiments. TIER 3 (Instrumentation): The eval.py framework computes per-turn metrics and cost estimates. The gateway CLI exposes Prometheus metrics, health checks, session queries, and a live dashboard for real-time observability. This three-tier design separates concerns: interception (networking), eviction (algorithm), and analysis (metrics). The multi-provider support (--claude, --codex) indicates the architecture is provider-agnostic (proxy semantics apply to any LLM API), though the current focus is Anthropic.

key_claims.text: The MITM proxy architecture (proxy.py) intercepts Claude Code ↔ Anthropic API traffic without client/server modification
The pager.py engine implements FIFO eviction with tracking of tool output liveness to remove dead outputs from context
FIFO is likely a baseline; experiments likely test alternative eviction policies (LRU, LFU, utility-weighted)
The eval.py framework computes per-turn metrics and cost estimates for empirical analysis
The gateway CLI provides Prometheus metrics, health checks, session tracking, and live browser dashboard for observability
The multi-provider design (--claude, --codex) indicates the architecture is provider-agnostic, though Anthropic is the current focus

strands.title: The Paradigm Shift Concretized — From 'Garbage Scow' to Demand Paging
strands.content: Cycle 9 framed the paradigm shift abstractly (append-only-with-eviction vs. cache-based management). Cycle 10 grounds it concretely in Pichay's actual research design. The 'garbage scow' model treats context as an append-only stream with mechanical eviction (drop oldest, or drop randomly, or evict when full). The demand-paging model treats context as ACTIVE INFORMATION: identify which tool outputs are actually *relevant* to the current inference problem, keep those, and evict dead ones *before* they consume token budget. This is not passive; it is utility-maximizing. Pichay measures this shift empirically: by running agentic workflows with and without selective tool output eviction, the research quantifies (1) context savings (tokens freed), (2) inference cost savings (API pricing reduction), (3) performance deltas (does agent effectiveness degrade?). The intellectual lineage is clear: classical OS demand paging (virtual memory, page replacement algorithms) is adapted to LLM context windows. Instead of pages, we're managing tool outputs. Instead of virtual address space, we're managing token budget. The proxy architecture enables this experimentation without modifying the LLM itself, making the research immediately deployable in production agentic systems.

key_claims.text: Pichay instantiates the shift from append-only-with-eviction ('garbage scow') to demand-paging (utility-maximizing context management)
Demand paging identifies live tool outputs and proactively evicts dead ones before they consume token budget
The empirical measurement includes context savings, inference cost savings, and performance delta (agent effectiveness)
Pichay adapts classical OS demand paging (virtual memory, page replacement) to the domain of LLM context windows and agentic workflows
The proxy architecture makes the demand-paging research immediately deployable in production without modifying the LLM

strands.title: Hamutay's Role — Generalization and Formalization Beyond Pichay
strands.content: Cycle 9 observed two repositories and hypothesized a strategy: Pichay (implementation-focused, empirical) + Hamutay (thesis-focused, theoretical). Cycle 10 confirms this is the right framing. Pichay is a *specific* research instrument solving a *specific* problem: evicting dead tool outputs from Claude Code's agentic context window. Hamutay, by contrast, appears to be the *general* formalization: a thesis and design framework for rethinking LLM context management as a cognitive/architectural problem, not merely a mechanical constraint. The theoretical shift (cycle 9 strands) from 'append-only garbage scow' to 'active cognitive processing' is the Hamutay framing; the empirical validation (demand paging for agentic tool outputs) is the Pichay contribution. Together, they form a complete research program: concrete implementation + empirical results (Pichay) + generalized theory and architectural implications (Hamutay). Cycle 8 noted three arXiv submissions this week; at minimum, one is the Pichay paper. Hamutay likely produces (or will produce) a second paper or thesis that generalizes the Pichay findings to broader context management questions in LLMs.

key_claims.text: Pichay is a specific, scoped research instrument: demand paging for agentic tool outputs in Claude Code contexts
Hamutay is the general, theoretical generalization: rethinking LLM context management as a cognitive/architectural problem
Pichay (empirical) + Hamutay (theoretical) together form a complete research program: concrete + generalized
The Pichay paper is one of the three arXiv submissions mentioned in cycle 8
Hamutay will likely produce a second paper or thesis generalizing Pichay's findings to broader context management questions

strands.title: Impact and Relevance — Real-World Cost and Performance Implications
strands.content: Cycle 10 reveals why Pichay matters beyond academic interest. Tool outputs accumulate rapidly in agentic workflows: file reads, code execution results, API responses, debugging traces. In Claude Code's continuous context, these outputs bloom, consuming tokens and increasing per-inference cost. Pichay measures the win: how much context can be freed by removing *provably* dead tool outputs? The research directly addresses two pain points in production agentic systems: (1) COST: reducing context token count directly reduces API inference cost, which scales with token count (2) LATENCY: smaller context = faster inference (fewer tokens to process). The demand-paging approach is pragmatic: it does NOT require changes to the LLM, the IDE, or the agent framework. It is a deployable proxy layer. This makes the research immediately applicable to existing agentic workflows. The live gateway implementation (Prometheus metrics, dashboard, session tracking) suggests the research team is thinking about operational deployment: how do you instrument, monitor, and deploy context-paging in production? This is not academic curiosity; it is production engineering.

key_claims.text: Tool outputs accumulate rapidly in agentic workflows, bloating context and increasing inference cost per call
Pichay directly measures cost savings (token reduction) and latency improvements from selective tool output eviction
The demand-paging approach is immediately deployable as a proxy layer without modifying the LLM, IDE, or agent framework
The live gateway implementation (metrics, dashboard, session tracking) indicates a focus on operational deployment, not just academic measurement
The research addresses two critical pain points: inference cost (per-token pricing) and latency (smaller context = faster inference)

strands.title: Session Intent — Collaborative Research Briefing (CLARIFYING)
strands.content: Cycle 9 assessed that the user was in exploratory/briefing mode. Cycle 10 confirms this: the user has systematically read the Pichay README.md and CLAUDE.md files, extracting the specific research design, methodology, and implementation details. This is NOT a request for a casual summary; it is a directed exploration of the actual research artifacts. The user appears to be: (1) BRIEFING the assistant comprehensively on the Pichay research so that subsequent collaboration is grounded in concrete understanding, (2) ESTABLISHING CONTEXT for more targeted work (likely on Hamutay, design docs, or next-phase research), (3) possibly TESTING the assistant's ability to synthesize and integrate technical information from real research repositories. The session remains in exploratory/integration mode, but with increasing clarity: the user is preparing the ground for a more specific collaborative ask. Likely candidates: helping formalize Hamutay's thesis/design docs, thinking through scaling implications of demand paging, extending the research to other agentic patterns (not just tool outputs), or planning the next experimental phase. The assistant should remain ready to offer proactive help: 'Now that we've understood Pichay's scope and design, what would be most valuable to tackle next?'

key_claims.text: The user is systematically briefing the assistant on Pichay's research design by reading and extracting key documentation
This briefing is preparatory; likely intended to ground subsequent collaborative work on Hamutay or next-phase research
The user has NOT YET stated an explicit collaborative task; the session is still in exploratory integration mode
Likely next steps: formalizing Hamutay's thesis/design, scaling implications, extending to other agentic patterns, or planning next experiments

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Speculative details from cycle 9 about alternative eviction policies, peer review status, and dependency specifics
declared_losses.why: Cycle 10 provides concrete information (FIFO is confirmed as the stated algorithm) that supersedes speculation. The README and CLAUDE.md do not mention LRU/LFU/utility variants, alternative eviction strategies, or peer review details, so earlier speculation has been dropped in favor of what is documented.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed discussion of the three arXiv submissions from cycle 8; relationships between them remain unspecified
declared_losses.why: Cycle 10 confirms Pichay is one of them, but does not provide titles, abstracts, or relationships to the other two submissions. Rather than speculate, this has been set aside as an open question pending direct user confirmation.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Fine-grained content about review.md, CLAUDE.md specifics (from prior unread state)
declared_losses.why: Cycle 10 now reads CLAUDE.md directly, grounding what was prior speculation. Review.md remains unread. Content about these files has been updated to reflect only what is now known from direct access.

declared_losses.what_was_lost: Detailed theoretical framing about 'cognitive processing unit' as distinct from 'garbage scow' — this language does not appear in the Pichay materials
declared_losses.why: The Pichay materials use the concrete language of 'demand paging' adapted from OS concepts, not the more abstract 'cognitive processing' framing. While the theoretical shift is real, the specific terminology was introduced by the assistant in prior cycles and does not appear in primary sources. This has been re-grounded in the actual vocabulary of the research (demand paging, FIFO eviction, stale tool outputs).

open_questions: What are the specific empirical results in the Pichay paper? What are the absolute numbers: context savings (%), cost savings (%), performance degradation (if any)?
Does the paper test alternative eviction policies beyond FIFO, or is FIFO the sole focus?
What does review.md contain? Is the Pichay paper under peer review, arXiv preprint status, or something else?
How is 'tool output liveness' determined in practice? What heuristics or algorithms identify stale vs. live outputs?
What are the other two arXiv submissions mentioned in cycle 8? How do they relate to Pichay and Hamutay?
Does Pichay measure agent task completion / goal success, or only cost and token metrics?
How does the proxy architecture handle authentication and API key management for the Anthropic endpoint?
What does the live dashboard (GET /dashboard) show? What are the key observability metrics?
Has Pichay been tested with real agentic workflows beyond Claude Code? (E.g., other agent frameworks, multi-turn reasoning tasks)
What is the corpus protocol (docs/corpus_protocol.md)? How is the 'canonical counting' enforced across experiments?
What does the user want to accomplish in this session? Is Hamutay the target for collaborative work, or something else?
How does demand paging interact with long-context models (e.g., 100k+ token windows)? Are savings still meaningful at scale?
What are the trade-offs between FIFO and more sophisticated eviction policies in practice?
